classification: Exactly one of: "RCT", "RWE", or "INELIGIBLE". RCT = an interventional, experimental study that randomly allocates participants to intervention vs control under controlled trial conditions. RWE = clinical evidence that measures the effectiveness, safety, or health outcomes of the intervention using observational study designs or real-world data sources (prospective/retrospective cohort, case-control, registry-based, pragmatic non-randomized, claims, electronic health records, app/device usage data) collected in routine clinical practice. Being conducted in a real-world setting (e.g. a hospital department) is NOT sufficient to be RWE - the study must analyze real-world/observational data to evaluate clinical outcomes. INELIGIBLE = anything that is neither a primary RCT nor a primary RWE study. This explicitly includes usability, feasibility, acceptability, pilot, or proof-of-concept studies and technical/human-factors evaluations (e.g. interface design, cognitive load, technical proficiency, user experience) whose purpose is to inform or select a future trial rather than measure clinical effectiveness or real-world health impact - these are INELIGIBLE even when run in a real-world setting. Also INELIGIBLE: narrative or systematic reviews, meta-analyses, study protocols without results, editorials, commentaries, letters, case reports, and qualitative-only studies.
study_design: Short, specific label for the actual design detected, e.g. 'randomized controlled trial', 'prospective cohort', 'retrospective registry analysis', 'systematic review', 'study protocol'.
confidence: Integer from 0 to 100 expressing how certain you are about the classification.
reason: One or two sentences justifying the classification, citing the design features that drove the decision (randomization and control vs observational/real-world setting vs not a primary study).
